description_en:
In 2019, the Canadian Passport Order was amended to authorize the use of automated decision-making, enabling the Passport Program to begin automated processing through the Global Case Management System.  Automated decision-making is only used for routine, low risk cases resulting in positive decisions (e.g. adult passport renewals). Cases that are considered more complex, including all first-time applicants, child applications, and some other cases, will drop out for manual officer review and decision-making. All decisions to refuse, revoke, or cancel a passport will also continue to be made by an officer. 
•	First, the applicant’s identity is verified. 
•	Second, validation of the applicant’s eligibility to a passport is completed, e.g.  to ensure the applicant is a Canadian citizen. 
•	Third, confirmation of the applicant’s entitlement to a passport is completed, e.g.  whether there are any restrictions that could impact the applicant's right to a passport. 
Under the current process for passport renewals, passport officers run a series of manual checks on applications, based on the information that the applicant submits in their application and information available in the system to make a decision on issuance of the passport. The implementation of this automation aims to improve the efficiency of these checks through automated business rules that reflect the manual steps currently performed by a passport officer.

The issuance of a passport will result in impacts that last years (the validity of the passport can be up to 10 years) unless there is a reason for revocation or cancellation of the passport. ", "impact9": "item1-1", "impact10": "The automation does not include refusals to issue a passport therefore the impacts of automation have little to no negative impact on the rights or freedoms of the individuals. Given automated positive decisions of low risk, low complexity renewal applications are likely to be made more quickly than the current process, automation is likely to result in a net positive impact on the rights and freedoms of individuals by facilitating their Section 6 Mobility Rights. ", "impact11": "item1-1", "impact12": "The automation does not include refusals to issue a passport therefore the impacts of automation have "little to no negative impact" on heath and well-being. Given automated positive decisions of low risk, low complexity renewal applications are likely to be made more quickly than the current process, automation is likely to result in a net positive impact on individuals' health and well being. 
", "impact13": "item1-1", "impact14": "The automation does not include refusals to issue a passport therefore the impacts of automation have "little to no negative impact" on the economic interests of individuals. Given automated positive decisions of low risk, low complexity renewal applications are likely to be made more quickly than the current process, automation is likely to result in a net positive impact on individuals' economic interests, especially those who rely on frequent business travel. ", "impact15": "item1-1", "impact16": " Passport decisions, either positive or negative, are unlikely to have impacts on environmental sustainability.
